heading: A
raw: I love being a beautiful woman when I look at myself in the mirror. Layal Abboud, June 15, 2017; Al Kahera Walnas
clean: I love being a beautiful woman when I look at myself in the mirror. Layal Abboud, June 15, 2017; Al Kahera Walnas

heading: A
raw: Omnis mundi creatura Quasi liber et pictura Nobis est, et speculum. All creation, like a book or a picture, is a mirror to us. Alain de Lille, in De Incarnatione Christi (c. 1170)
clean: Omnis mundi creatura Quasi liber et pictura Nobis est, et speculum. All creation, like a book or a picture, is a mirror to us. Alain de Lille, in De Incarnatione Christi (c. 1170)

heading: A
raw: Come you lost Atoms to your Centre draw,And be the Eternal Mirror that you saw:Rays that have wander'd into Darkness wideReturn and back into your Sun subside. Attar, in The Conference of the Birds (1177)
clean: Come you lost Atoms to your Centre draw,And be the Eternal Mirror that you saw:Rays that have wander'd into Darkness wideReturn and back into your Sun subside. Attar, in The Conference of the Birds (1177)

heading: A
raw: Mirrorsare the perfect lovers. Margaret Atwood, in You are Happy (1974), 'Tricks with Mirrors'
clean: Mirrorsare the perfect lovers. Margaret Atwood, in You are Happy (1974), 'Tricks with Mirrors'

heading: A
raw: To live in prison is to live without mirrors. To live without mirrors is to live without the self. Margaret Atwood, in Two-Headed Poems (1978), 'Marrying the Hangman'
clean: To live in prison is to live without mirrors. To live without mirrors is to live without the self. Margaret Atwood, in Two-Headed Poems (1978), 'Marrying the Hangman'

heading: B
raw: Unsourced variant: "I wanted to rub humanity's face in its own vomit and force it to look in the mirror."
clean: I wanted to rub humanity's face in its own vomit and force it to look in the mirror.

heading: B
raw: Le Dandy doit aspirer à être sublime, sans interruption. Il doit vivre et dormir devant un miroir. The dandy should try to be sublime without interruption. He should live and sleep before a mirror. Charles Baudelaire, in Mon cœur mis à nu (1887)
clean: Le Dandy doit aspirer à être sublime, sans interruption. Il doit vivre et dormir devant un miroir. The dandy should try to be sublime without interruption. He should live and sleep before a mirror. Charles Baudelaire, in Mon cœur mis à nu (1887)

heading: B
raw: I want to confess as best I can, but my heart is void. The void is a mirror. I see my face and feel loathing and horror. My indifference to men has shut me out. I live now in a world of ghosts, a prisoner in my dreams. Ingmar Bergman, in The Seventh Seal (1957)
clean: I want to confess as best I can, but my heart is void. The void is a mirror. I see my face and feel loathing and horror. My indifference to men has shut me out. I live now in a world of ghosts, a prisoner in my dreams. Ingmar Bergman, in The Seventh Seal (1957)

heading: B
raw: LOOKING-GLASS, n. A vitreous plane upon which to display a fleeting show for man's disillusion given. Ambrose Bierce, The Cynic's Dictionary (1906); republished as The Devil's Dictionary (1911).
clean: LOOKING-GLASS, n. A vitreous plane upon which to display a fleeting show for man's disillusion given. Ambrose Bierce, The Cynic's Dictionary (1906); republished as The Devil's Dictionary (1911).

heading: B
raw: Variant translation: Mirrors and copulation are obscene, for they increase the numbers of mankind.
clean: Mirrors and copulation are obscene, for they increase the numbers of mankind.

heading: B
raw: I owe the discovery of Uqbar to the conjunction of a mirror and an encyclopedia. Jorge Luis Borges, in Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius (1940), First lines
clean: I owe the discovery of Uqbar to the conjunction of a mirror and an encyclopedia. Jorge Luis Borges, in Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius (1940), First lines

heading: B
raw: One of the heresiarchs of Uqbar had stated that mirrors and copulation are abominable, since they both multiply the numbers of man. Jorge Luis Borges, in Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius (1940)
clean: One of the heresiarchs of Uqbar had stated that mirrors and copulation are abominable, since they both multiply the numbers of man. Jorge Luis Borges, in Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius (1940)

heading: B
raw: Variant translation: For one of those gnostics, the visible universe was an illusion or, more precisely, a sophism. Mirrors and fatherhood are abominable because they multiply it and extend it.
clean: For one of those gnostics, the visible universe was an illusion or, more precisely, a sophism. Mirrors and fatherhood are abominable because they multiply it and extend it.

heading: B
raw: If art reflects life, it does so with special mirrors. Bertolt Brecht, in A Short Organum for the Theatre (1949), ¶ 73
clean: If art reflects life, it does so with special mirrors. Bertolt Brecht, in A Short Organum for the Theatre (1949), ¶ 73

heading: B
raw: All Faith is false, all Faith is true: Truth is the shattered mirror strown In myriad bits; while each believes his little bit the whole to own. Richard Francis Burton, in The Kasîdah of Hâjî Abdû El-Yezdî (1870), VI
clean: All Faith is false, all Faith is true: Truth is the shattered mirror strown In myriad bits; while each believes his little bit the whole to own. Richard Francis Burton, in The Kasîdah of Hâjî Abdû El-Yezdî (1870), VI

heading: C
raw: Literature is a vast bazaar where customers come to purchase everything except mirrors. James Branch Cabell, in The Certain Hour (1916)
clean: Literature is a vast bazaar where customers come to purchase everything except mirrors. James Branch Cabell, in The Certain Hour (1916)

heading: D
raw: I never entertained the dreadful thought that my face was anything other than good and fair until, in an act of revelation, I picked up a mirror. Dafydd ap Gwilym, in "Y Drych" [The Mirror], line 1; translation from Bards and Heroes (1989) by Carl Lofmark, p. 96
clean: I never entertained the dreadful thought that my face was anything other than good and fair until, in an act of revelation, I picked up a mirror. Dafydd ap Gwilym, in "Y Drych" , line 1; translation from Bards and Heroes (1989) by Carl Lofmark, p. 96

heading: G
raw: People think a soul mate is your perfect fit, and that's what everyone wants. But a true soul mate is a mirror, the person who shows you everything that is holding you back, the person who brings you to your own attention so you can change your life. Elizabeth Gilbert, in Eat, Pray, Love (2006)
clean: People think a soul mate is your perfect fit, and that's what everyone wants. But a true soul mate is a mirror, the person who shows you everything that is holding you back, the person who brings you to your own attention so you can change your life. Elizabeth Gilbert, in Eat, Pray, Love (2006)

heading: H
raw: Mirrors on the ceiling, the pink champagne on ice and she said: "We are all just prisoners here; of our own device." Don Henley, "Hotel California" (1976), Hotel California, Asylum Records
clean: Mirrors on the ceiling, the pink champagne on ice and she said: "We are all just prisoners here; of our own device." Don Henley, "Hotel California" (1976), Hotel California, Asylum Records

heading: K
raw: Everyone is a mirror image of yourself — your own thinking coming back at you. Byron Katie, in Loving What Is : Four Questions That Can Change Your Life (2002)
clean: Everyone is a mirror image of yourself - your own thinking coming back at you. Byron Katie, in Loving What Is : Four Questions That Can Change Your Life (2002)

heading: L
raw: Il accusait toujours les miroirs d'être faux. He was always blaming mirrors for being untrue. Jean de La Fontaine, in Fables (1668), Part I, no. 11, 'L'homme et son image', as quoted in Chambers Dictionary of Quotations (1997), p. 574
clean: Il accusait toujours les miroirs d'être faux. He was always blaming mirrors for being untrue. Jean de La Fontaine, in Fables (1668), Part I, no. 11, 'L'homme et son image', as quoted in Chambers Dictionary of Quotations (1997), p. 574

heading: L
raw: It may be said that not only the soul, the mirror of an indestructible universe, is indestructible, but also the animal itself, though its mechanism may often perish in part and take off or put on an organic slough. Gottfried Leibniz, in La monadologie (1714) § 77
clean: It may be said that not only the soul, the mirror of an indestructible universe, is indestructible, but also the animal itself, though its mechanism may often perish in part and take off or put on an organic slough. Gottfried Leibniz, in La monadologie (1714) § 77

heading: L
raw: The painter who draws merely by practice and by eye, without any reason, is like a mirror which copies every thing placed in front of it without being conscious of their existence. Leonardo da Vinci, in The Notebooks of Leonardo da Vinci Vol. I, as translated by Jean Paul Richter (1888)
clean: The painter who draws merely by practice and by eye, without any reason, is like a mirror which copies every thing placed in front of it without being conscious of their existence. Leonardo da Vinci, in The Notebooks of Leonardo da Vinci Vol. I, as translated by Jean Paul Richter (1888)

heading: L
raw: A book is a mirror: if an ape looks into it an apostle is hardly likely to look out. We have no words for speaking of wisdom to the stupid. He who understands the wise is wise already. Georg Christoph Lichtenberg, in Aphorisms Notebook E 49
clean: if an ape looks into it an apostle is hardly likely to look out. We have no words for speaking of wisdom to the stupid. He who understands the wise is wise already. Georg Christoph Lichtenberg, in Aphorisms Notebook E 49

heading: L
raw: Variant translations of first portion: A book is a mirror: If an ape peers into it, you can't expect an apostle to look out.
clean: A book is a mirror: If an ape peers into it, you can't expect an apostle to look out.

heading: L
raw: A man has only one escape from his old self: to see a different self — in the mirror of some woman's eyes. Clare Boothe Luce, in The Women (1936) Act I
clean: A man has only one escape from his old self: to see a different self - in the mirror of some woman's eyes. Clare Boothe Luce, in The Women (1936) Act I

heading: M

heading: O
raw: Life is for each man a solitary cell whose walls are mirrors. Eugene O'Neill, in Lazarus Laughed (1927), ACT II, scene i

heading: R
raw: Universe is an empty mirror. Life is just the reflection of your deep thoughts. Amit Ray in Enlightenment Step by Step (2016)

heading: R
raw: The past reflects eternally between two mirrors — the bright mirror of words and deeds, and the dark one, full of things we didn't do or say. Gregory David Roberts, in Shantaram (2003)
clean: The past reflects eternally between two mirrors - the bright mirror of words and deeds, and the dark one, full of things we didn't do or say. Gregory David Roberts, in Shantaram (2003)

heading: S
raw: Each day the vain Queen consulted her Magic Mirror, "Magic Mirror on the wall, who is the fairest one of all?"… and as long as the Mirror answered, "You are the fairest of them all," Snow White was safe from the Queen's cruel jealousy. Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs (1937 film)

heading: W

heading: W
raw: There are two ways of spreading light: to beThe candle or the mirror that reflects it. Edith Wharton, in "Vesalius in Zante (1564)", in North American Review (November 1902), p. 631
